caption.text: In the early twentieth century, this area of Boston’s South End was densely populated and very mixed-use. It included multiple schools, both religious and secular, as well as two theaters and many places of worship.

Boston Latin School was founded in the 1600s as the first public school in what would become the United States. The English High School was founded in 1821, an early public high school in the country. Both schools only admitted boys until 1972. Both have moved around the city over time. For the years between 1866 and 1944, the schools shared a building, moving to this location together in 1881, and were generally seen as counterparts. Boston Latin was a college preparatory school while The English High School specialized in educating working-class boys for business, mechanics, and engineering trades.
This building near the Latin and English High Schools has an oddly circular footprint because several decades earlier…
…it was originally built as the Battle of Gettysburg Cyclorama.

This building housed a massive circular painting depicting the Battle of Gettysburg in hyper-realistic detail in an attempt to immerse the viewer in the painting as if they were at the battle personally. As this photograph shows, it was a very popular form of entertainment at the time…
